Category: Media & Entertainment: Publishing, Film, Advertising & Communications, Gaming
Description: Legal ambiguity in publishing, film, and media.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with how creative industries such as publishing, film, or advertising operate is helpful, though no legal training or technical knowledge of artificial intelligence is required.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key legal grey areas surrounding AI-generated content in publishing, film, and media, and explain how current copyright frameworks apply (or fail to apply) to content produced with AI tools. You will also be able to apply practical strategies to assess ownership risks in creative projects across advertising, communications, and gaming contexts.

lessons.Description: As artificial intelligence becomes increasingly embedded in creative industries, questions around ownership and copyright are becoming impossible to ignore. This lesson explores the legal grey areas that arise when AI tools are used to generate written content, images, scripts, and other media in publishing, film, and broadcasting. You will learn how current copyright law struggles to keep pace with AI-generated work, and why the question of "who owns it?" is rarely straightforward. Drawing on real-world examples from the UK and beyond, the lesson offers a clear and practical introduction to the key issues facing creators, commissioners, and organisations working with AI today.
lessons.content: Legal ambiguity in publishing, film, and media.

slides.subtitle: Navigating copyright in the age of AI
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools now generate text, images, music, and video at scale
• Copyright law was not designed with AI-generated content in mind
• Publishers, filmmakers, and media companies face real legal uncertainty
• Who owns the output, the user, the developer, or nobody
• The answer varies depending on jurisdiction and context

slides.subtitle: Navigating Uncharted Legal Territory Today
slides.slidecontent:
• Most countries offer no clear copyright protection for purely AI-generated content
• Human authorship still the central requirement in UK and US law
• Courts and legislators actively debating where the boundaries lie
• A fast-moving area
• Publishers, studios, and platforms already facing real disputes over ownership rights
• Existing frameworks written long before generative AI existed

slides.subtitle: Ownership depends on input and intent.
slides.slidecontent:
• Who created the prompt?
• What tool was used?
• Human input shapes ownership
• Intent matters legally
• Output varies by platform

slides.subtitle: Where traditional authorship rules fall short
slides.slidecontent:
• No human, no copyright
• AI creates, law hesitates
• Authorship requires human intent
• Existing rules simply don't fit
• Ownership stays legally unclear

slides.subtitle: Where creativity meets legal uncertainty
slides.slidecontent:
• No human, no copyright
• AI art, legal grey area
• Films, music, books affected
• Ownership stays unresolved
• Laws lag behind technology

slides.subtitle: Where law struggles to keep pace
slides.slidecontent:
• Most copyright law written long before generative tools existed
• No human author, no automatic copyright protection in many jurisdictions
• The UK treats computer-generated works differently, offering limited protection to the person who arranged the creation
• A grey area
• Courts and legislators still debating where creative credit truly begins and ends
• Ownership claims remain legally fragile without clear human input on record

slides.subtitle: Strategies for protecting your creative work
slides.slidecontent:
• Document your creative process from the very start, saving drafts, notes, and version histories as evidence of human authorship
• Register your work with relevant copyright bodies where possible
• Disclose AI involvement clearly in contracts and submissions
• Consult a media lawyer before signing deals involving AI-assisted content
• Keep records

slides.subtitle: Essential points to carry forward today
slides.slidecontent:
• AI authorship remains legally unclear
• Humans must add creative input
• Register and document your work
• Contracts should address AI use
• Laws are changing fast
• Seek specialist legal advice

scripts.script: Welcome to this lesson on AI-generated content and copyright. Over the past few years, AI tools have become remarkably capable. They can write articles, compose music, generate images, and even produce video, often in seconds. But here is where things get complicated. Copyright law was built around the idea of human creativity, and nobody really anticipated a world where machines could produce creative work at this scale. That creates genuine uncertainty for anyone working in publishing, film, media, or really any creative industry. The central question we are going to explore is a deceptively simple one: who actually owns the content that AI produces? Is it the person who prompted the tool? The company that built it? Or does the content belong to nobody at all? As you will discover, there is no single answer. It depends on where you are in the world and how the content was created. So let us dig in and start making sense of it all.

So where do things actually stand right now, legally speaking? Well, the honest answer is that it is genuinely unclear. In most countries, including the UK and the United States, copyright law has traditionally required a human author. If a person did not create it, the law largely does not protect it. But generative AI has thrown that assumption into question, and courts and governments are actively trying to work out what the rules should be. This is not a settled area of law, it is moving very quickly. And it is not just a theoretical debate either. Publishers, film studios, and online platforms are already dealing with real disputes about who owns what when AI is involved. The important thing to understand is that the legal frameworks we have today were written long before tools like these even existed. So we are essentially trying to apply old rules to a completely new situation, and that creates a lot of uncertainty.

So when we talk about who actually owns AI-generated content, the answer is rarely straightforward, and it almost always comes down to a few key factors. First, who wrote the prompt? The person guiding the AI with their instructions is often the most important figure in any ownership conversation. But it is not just about the person; it also matters which tool they used, because different platforms have very different terms of service, and those terms directly affect what you can and cannot claim as your own. The more human creativity and judgement that goes into shaping the output, the stronger the case for ownership tends to be. And intent plays a role too. Courts and legal frameworks are starting to ask whether a human made meaningful creative decisions along the way. So the short version is this: ownership of AI-generated content is not automatic. It depends on your input, your choices, and the platform you are working with.

So here's where things start to get really interesting. Copyright law, as it stands, was built around one core assumption: that a human being created the work. A person sat down, made creative choices, and produced something original. But what happens when an AI does that instead? Well, the law gets very uncomfortable very quickly. Most legal systems, including here in the UK, require a human author for copyright to exist in the first place. There's no provision for a machine to hold rights, and crucially, there's no recognised human intent behind what the AI produces on its own. So the existing rules, which were written long before any of this technology existed, simply don't stretch far enough to cover it. That leaves us in a genuinely uncertain position where AI can generate something that looks and feels like creative work, but nobody can clearly say who, if anyone, actually owns it. That's the problem we're going to keep unpacking throughout this course.

So here's where things get really interesting, especially if you work in any kind of creative field. The core legal principle in most countries, including the UK, is that copyright only exists if a human being created the work. If an AI generates an image, writes a story, or composes a piece of music entirely on its own, there is currently no copyright protection attached to it. That puts AI-generated art, film, literature, and music in a genuine legal grey area, because the question of who actually owns that output simply has no clear answer right now. Could it be the person who typed the prompt? The company that built the AI? Nobody at all? Courts and lawmakers are still working that out, and the honest truth is that the law is struggling to keep pace with how quickly this technology is moving. So if you are creating or commissioning AI-generated content professionally, you need to be aware that the ownership question is very much still open.

Here is where things get genuinely complicated. Most copyright law was written decades ago, long before anyone imagined tools that could generate images, text, or music at the click of a button. The fundamental assumption baked into these laws is that a human being created the work, and without that human author, automatic copyright protection simply does not apply in many countries. The UK does take a slightly different approach, offering some limited protection to the person who arranged or set up the creation process, but even that is quite narrow. Globally, this is still very much a grey area. Courts are debating it, legislators are debating it, and nobody has landed on a definitive answer yet. What that means practically is that if you are relying on AI-generated content and you want to claim ownership, your legal position can be surprisingly fragile unless you have clear evidence of meaningful human involvement in the creative process.

So, the law around AI-generated content is still catching up, but that doesn't mean you're powerless. There are some sensible, practical steps you can take right now to protect yourself. First, get into the habit of documenting your creative process from the very beginning. Save your drafts, your notes, your version histories, because that paper trail is what demonstrates human involvement. If copyright registration is available in your territory, use it. It's a small effort that can make a big difference later. When you're working with clients or submitting work, be upfront about any AI involvement in your contracts and submissions. Transparency now prevents disputes down the line. And if you're about to sign a deal involving AI-assisted content, please speak to a media lawyer first. I know that sounds like a big step, but it's genuinely worth it. Finally, keep records of everything. Good documentation is your best friend in a legal grey area like this one.

So let us bring everything together. The law still has not decided whether AI can be an author, which means if you want protection for your work, you need to make sure a human, ideally you, has made genuine creative decisions along the way. Do not just accept whatever the AI produces. Shape it, direct it, make it yours. Beyond that, keep records of your process and register your work where you can, because documentation really does matter if a dispute ever arises. If you are working with clients or collaborators, make sure your contracts are clear about how AI is being used. And here is the thing to keep front of mind: this area of law is moving quickly. What is true today may shift within months. So if you are building a business or a creative practice around AI-generated content, please do speak with a specialist intellectual property solicitor. Getting proper advice now could save you a great deal of trouble later.

Who is this for: Content creators, copywriters, marketing managers, creative directors, brand managers, communications specialists, content managers, digital marketing professionals, advertising executives, social media managers
Team or Dept this is for: Marketing, Communications, Creative, Legal, Brand Management, Content Strategy, Digital Media
